speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the Rolf family that took you in in, in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was a family that-- only God could have created such people. Mrs. Rolf, whom I called Mom Rolf, really took us to her heart. And she insisted we move in there, even though it was difficult to get from Croydon-- we lived in Croydon-- for me to go to work every morning. So I still kept a little place for a while. But then I just moved in with her. And lo and behold, we got in touch with our family. And we started working on the papers to come and be reunited with our family in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What family did you have here in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only surviving aunts and uncles and most of my cousins were here in America. We had three uncles on my mother's side and an aunt, Aunt Rose, may she rest in peace, Uncle [? Gene, ?] Uncle [? Adolf, ?] and Uncle-- actually four, Uncle Bernie and Uncle Bernard. And on my father's side we had two uncles. And one of them passed away. But when the papers were arranged for us to come, Rosalyn came here about November or December of 1948. And our brothers were given permission to come from Czechoslovakia that January, the following January. One-- just at that time, Mom Rolf got very sick. And they found a lump in her brain. And I wanted her to get it operated on. And her rabbi-- the rabbi and the doctor came to talk to me, that Mom Rolf don't want to do it if I leave. So after a long discussion between her and Dad Rolf and the boys and the-- and the daughter, she agreed that if I stay, she'll have the operation.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister, Rosalyn, left. And I wanted to save up enough money to pay for my own trip to America. I didn't want my family to pay for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I agreed to stay. And I stayed in her hospital when she had the operation. And lo and behold, she got well. And finally, she said, three months later, you have saved my life. Now, if you really want to go, I won't stop you. I said well, Mom, now that I am here, I want to make sure you're going to stay healthy. And I want to save up enough money to pay for my fare. So she insisted that I put away-- and I got very little money. An apprentice doesn't make too much money in England. And she doubled-- I didn't know it, but she doubled everything I saved. And finally, in December, a whole bunch of us survivors-- kids-- got on a boat, on the Queen Mary, to come to America. And of course, it, it was a dream of all dreams come true. It was a dream of our parents. It was my dream. And finally, we landed here December 16, 1949. Just to see the just to see the shores of America was truly a miracle of all miracles. And when we got there, my aunt, may she rest in peace, sent somebody to look for me. But he couldn't pronounce Gross. Finally, I persuaded one of the guys. And he took me. And she looked up. And she says, I expected you to be a foot taller. [LAUGHS] But she was absolutely a, a wonderful, wonderful, wonderful woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aunt Rose, may she rest in peace. She was a-- quite an artist. And I got some art pieces from her. And when she got ill, I went to see her every month to Miami to be with her. And when my brother found a girl he wanted to marry, my brother Ben, he decided not to get married till I got here. And he waited till I arrived-- the day after that. And I was the best man to his wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- no, in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, where he was a kosher butcher. In fact, they just celebrated their 50th anniversary. And his wife is [LAUGHS] nothing short of an angel. She's a wonderful woman. And her whole family were just absolutely great. And my uncles and aunts were simply beyond description how wonderful they were. I never accepted money. I never wanted to accept money. And I wanted to get a job right away. And I got a job in the knitting mill immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Pittsburgh?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ellwood City, Pennsylvania. And-- but at the same time, I was anxious to get back to my trade. So I got a hold of Jerry Hornstein that was already in Chicago. He found out somehow that I got here. And his spouse offered me a job right away. So I left for Chicago. And I worked there. But when the Korean War broke out, I was very anxious to join the US Army. And they found I had flat feet and a few other things. They didn't-- they were not anxious to take me either in Ellwood City or in Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I called up Aunt [? Hilde, ?] may she rest in peace, and she-- her brother was a Supreme Court judge in Pennsylvania. And she made a call to him and told him how anxious I was to join the US Army. And she called me back 10 days later. Come on, you've got a greeting from the president. You're being drafted. So I came back to Ellwood City and joined the US Army. And probably the happiest two years of my life, because I fulfilled two goals. Number one, to give something of myself to America because I was so grateful. And number two, it gave me an opportunity to get to know the American people and get a little bit of an education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when I was stationed in Fort Bragg, North Carolina, I got to meet a lot of people. When I was stationed in Fort Meade, same thing. And most of all, when was stationed in St. Louis, I managed to get a high school equivalent diploma. And above all, I managed to have two jobs in addition to my army jobs. And I saved up enough money to buy a car when I came out of the service. So I can have a car to join my brother. But I helped him start the business. And we got into the pre-cut home business. And, I worked very hard, hired a lot of refugees and hired a lot of people, trained them myself. And we became the largest pre-cut home manufacturers in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this back in Pennsylvania?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already in Youngstown, Ohio, Niles, Ohio. And we built up to 82 corporations [LAUGHS] that I was president of. And we were very, very successful, very successful. But you know, not knowing enough about American business-- and we set up finance companies. And then banks decided to go into the finance business. They put us out of business. And it didn't stop us. We continued on. And in the early 1960s, I had a dream that we wanted to build our own community. And we came out here to Georgia. And we acquired enough land to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to start building and developing and eventually a little house, something like 15,000 people. And we have already about 5,000 families living there. And we have shopping centers and office parks and rental property and single-family homes. And we built 16 lakes on the property to create a community that everybody will enjoy-- those that can afford better, as well as those that can afford very low. And we have been very fortunate there. We are very happy there. The people-- we started a soccer program, second to none. We were very-- my brother was very instrumental in the Atlanta Chiefs soccer team. And we have 16 soccer fields in our property. And I'm still active and try to play there. It's just a great community, wonderful people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to Atlanta, where you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was very fortunate. I met a girl on one of my trips to the bank. Everybody was anxious to introduce me to Jewish girls. And this guy came-- and first time when I met my wife, I wasn't interested at all. But I guess she went on a diet. And, and she became extremely attractive. And then the vice president came. You got to meet this girl. And I said, no, I don't have time. I got to go out of town.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I was leaving, this girl comes over to me. And she says, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] happy holidays. I said, how do you know it's my holidays? And she said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That's a happy new year.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I looked at her. And I said, I didn't know you were Jewish. And next thing I knew, I went on vacation. I said I found my girl. And I want to marry. And when I came back, I went to her family. And I said, I want to marry your daughter. And we've been blessed. We were married and had a wonderful, wonderful life. We had four children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three daughters-- three daughters, Etta, my oldest. She was named after my mother, may she rest in peace. And then our son, Benji. He was named after our uncle who had just died, one of them that brought us over. And then Stephanie and then Robin, youngest one. And we were-- we were in the process of moving down here to Atlanta. Unfortunately, our son was very involved, very productive. He wanted to work. And he out on a Bush Hawk. And unfortunately, the Bush Hawk chopped him up, killed him. So I had quite a hellish life to go through, losing my only son. And we somehow got our lives together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we tried to be as happy as we could. This was right at the time we were in the process of moving down here. And finally, we were settled. And we were a happy family again. And my wife, may she rest in peace, got involved in real estate. And we were doing a project in Atlanta. And we found her. She was a victim of rape, murder. And that, of course, devastated us. I still have a difficult time. And I always will have a difficult time about that, losing my only son and my wife that I loved. And we had a-- if there ever was a happy marriage, we had a happy marriage, even though we had our difficulties the first year or so. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Linda. And she was very, very involved in charitable work, so much so that when the people found out that she was murdered, they started a Chesed Society, which means doing good, in her honor, because she did everything without anybody knowing. She used to help-- and I'm talking non-denomination, Jewish as well as other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: To give you an example, when we were sitting shiva, I had two semi-blind people come over-- and that she was crying, an elderly lady and her daughter. What are they going to do, now that Linda is dead? I said, what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, well, Linda used to come every week, two or three times, and used to take us out shopping and bought us our food and made sure everything is taken care of. And I, I didn't even know about it. She did a lot of those things. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've always been involved in charity work. And I'm blessed. I've been-- I've been involved in many, many things. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --this was just another thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep on going after the death of your son and your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] The question is, how do I still keep on going? I try to make a life. My kids still have a terrible, terrible time. But somehow we make it. We're blessed with the strength that it is needed in order to keep going. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was blessed, and my oldest daughter got married. She had a baby, and-- which is our only granddaughter. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had our difficult time. The children are still suffering, because she was a good mother and a good wife and a good person. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- in fact, I decided to get married, because I thought that maybe my kids will snap out of it quicker. And it probably was a mistake, because she had two sons. And the middle son just did not want his mother to be married. And it was a very rough time. Finally, we got a divorce. And I, I can say that I find a lot of relief, a lot of satisfaction in helping others. I got involved in youth work. I got involved with Atlanta Jewish Federation. I was vice chairman and co-chairman of the builders division for raising the funds, general chairman for Israel Bonds. I was president of the survivors organization for quite a few years. I've been very active in that. And I do a lot of speaking in schools and Kiwanis Clubs and Rotaries.

speaker: interviewee
text: But most of all, working with kids-- I find that if I can help one child-- and it has nothing to do with what religion and what background-- I feel as though I help the whole world because the world really depends on the youth. Because someday, we might be taken. And who's going to be left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hopefully, the youth that will see that other people are good. Not just what they see on TV-- all the bad things, the murders and rapes and, and, and, and all the things that they shouldn't be seeing. They're exposed to too many bad things in life. And I, I think if we can expose them to more and more of the good in people, that maybe they'll start being good. And, and maybe the future generation will have a better life. But to give you a straight answer, I don't know. I don't know how I manage some time to keep going in spite of everything, because there's not a day that I don't miss my son. There's not a day that I don't miss my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's not a day that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I look at a kid. If he's blond, hey, this-- is this my son maybe? Or if, if a woman that's attractive looks similar to my wife, that, you know, I want to go over and maybe hug them and kiss them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm blessed with three wonderful daughters. And I'm also blessed with the fact that I have still five brothers and one sister alive. We have our differences, of course, like anybody else. But we're very supportive of each other because were still the only family that six brothers and one sister survived. And hopefully that as time goes on and, and, and we realize that we're getting a little older, we're going to start taking it a little bit easy and maybe start thinking about retiring. I am very fortunate. I met a very lovely lady in Miami whose daughter was a roommate with my daughter. And I've been seeing her ever since my wife left. And we really get together as often as you-- we can. I, I, I know that if there's anything I would need, my daughters would do it without a-- any questions-- or my brothers or my sister-in-laws for that matter. Even though we live in different areas, we're very close. We're very caring. That comes from background, from my parents, of course, and grandparents. I know-- I remember my grandfather, may he rest in peace, was the same way. But there's a lot of good that I've seen in people, except they don't get the publicity that the bad things get. For instance, I get [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 10,000, 15,000 letters every year from students. And they tell me some of their problems. And they ask me some of their questions, how they could overcome their difficulties. And we somehow-- at least I give them my opinion what I think they ought to do. And it's so wonderful that 10, 15 years later, those same kids are successful. They become doctors. They become lawyers. They become business people. They'll come up to me and hug me and kiss me and tell me how grateful they are that I managed to make them realize that their problems is not the end of the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell your own children when they were growing up about your experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I could not speak about it. But when my daughters found out that the Catholic priest-- who later on became a cardinal, Cardinal Malone-- got me to speak in school, my children insisted that I got to come to their school. So I went there and spoke to the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I could never talk to my kids about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that you first started talking about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about 25 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that in raising your own children, there was anything in the way that you raised them that was directly related to you being in the Holocaust? What did you want to teach them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know whether it was the directly related so much to the Holocaust, as it was directly related to the way my mother and father would have wanted them to be raised. I taught them to love. I taught them to be good people-- at least I wanted to. And they turned out very good. I'm very proud of them. And my youngest daughter's working for the SEC in Washington. My middle daughter just got remarried to a very nice young man, whose mother is a survivor. And my oldest daughter-- married to a wonderful, caring person with service merchandise. And, and, and I don't think she could have ever met anybody any better than he is. And he's very good to me. And he's very good to her daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have really a lot to be grateful for. I, I feel that God has been very good to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I-- I've gone through all kinds of hell. And I've, I've lost something that cannot be described, my one and only son.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, I had my whole life based on him. Typical European upbringing-- you want him to take over your life, your business. And you want your children, your daughters, to take over. But none of my daughters wanted to be in the business, even though my oldest daughter was very good in real estate, but not with us. She was on her own. And I find that there's a lot more to life. In spite of what I've gone through, that I find there's a lot of good in people. It's just that what can you-- what do you take out of-- you can either take the good or the bad. If you get either extreme right or extreme left, whether it's fascism or communism, or any extremism-- Idi Amin, yes, any of those people, they take the bad out of people. And people become bad. And they become worse. But if you take the good out of people, they become good and better. I've seen some people that had some terrible upbringings, but they turned out to be very, very good. I know a lot of people that I, I help coach in soccer and when I was vice president of Rockdale Youth Soccer. And they turned out very, very good, even though the parents are not-- have been involved in drugs and everything else. Their brothers or sisters might have been in jail because of all kinds of bad things. But they turn out very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you get a lot of pleasure out of that. And really, I find that 95% of the people are very good. Really, they're good. But you can take that 5%, and they take over. Or the 2% that are bad, and they take over. And once they take over, all hell breaks lose-- Do you think the Holocaust could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? [SIGHS] Do I think the Holocaust can happen again? Unfortunately, the answer is yes. I wish it would be different. But the answer is yes, it could happen again. Because we never thought it could happen. We never thought-- Hitler only got 3%, 3.5% of the vote in Germany when he took over, when his party took over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You can see what's happening here. You see in Russia the extremists coming up. You can see in America extremists coming up. You, you had-- you had a guy in, in-- not too long ago admitted that he was a right-wing guy. And fortunately, he run for a bigger officer and didn't make it. But he was a congressman. And you got some other congressmen who are not so good people and other senators, too. But fortunately, in America, because we have true freedom, true democracy, that we hope the goodness in people will prevail. And they will not permit that thing to happen. And I really feel as long as America will stay strong, they'll stop it from happening. But, you know, in our time, you have seen Iraq, seen Iran, seen-- just in our time what has happened. We have seen what happened, the tragedies in, in, in, in, in Serbia. It just tore me up. And my friend, Elie Wiesel, kept on going back. But we never paid attention to it, the atrocities that went on over there-- children being tortured, people being killed, mothers being killed. And we ignored it. And we should have not ignored it. That's why I'm scared that, yes, it could happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism when you came either to America or when you came to the South?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, we experienced it when we came to Conyers.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what form?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one guy came over to my brother. And he says, we don't like Jews and niggers. And we're going to get rid of you. It just so happens that a gentleman heard it. And he went to see the sheriff. And the sheriff went to see him. And he told me, you touch anything on Mr. Gross, and I'll personally take care of you. And he never denied it. And it happened in the service to me when I was stationed at Fort Bragg-- one of the Italian fascist guys that was an American. And he openly said, I hate Jews. Mussolini was right. They should have killed them all. I took care of him, too. [LAUGHS] But yes, I've seen-- I've run across many, many incidents of anti-Semitism and hate, not only against Jews, but against women and against other minorities. And they knew that I will not stand-- I will not tolerate it. So, yes, I've seen it-- I've seen it happen, unfortunately, too many times. But luckily, this is America. And we have been able to overcome it. And we have a lot of good people. Because I said, basically, 95% of the people are good. The unfortunate part about it-- most of them are apathetic. And that's what brings on extremism. That's what brings on atrocities. And that's what scares me.
